name: Protactinium
name_origin: From the Greek 'protos' (first) and actinium, because it decays into actinium.

discovery.discoverer: Kasimir Fajans, Oswald Helmuth Gohring (Pa-234m); Otto Hahn, Lise Meitner, Frederick Soddy, John Cranston (Pa-231)

occurrence.abundance_summary: Extremely rare in nature; about 0.1 ppt of uranium ores. Pitchblende from the Democratic Republic of Congo contains the highest concentrations, around 3 ppm.
states: trace in pitchblende and uranium ores

compounds.name: Protactinium pentoxide
compounds.summary: White solid, the most stable oxide of protactinium; isostructural with the pentoxides of niobium and tantalum.

compounds.name: Protactinium pentafluoride
compounds.summary: Volatile white solid used as a precursor in protactinium chemistry and in early studies of actinide bonding.

compounds.name: Protactinium pentachloride
compounds.summary: Yellow crystalline solid useful in vapor-phase studies and as a precursor for further protactinium compounds.

uses: Scientific research into actinide chemistry and nuclear physics
Tracer in oceanographic dating, particularly the Pa-231/Th-230 ratio for marine sediment chronology
reactivity_summary: A bright silvery metal that resists corrosion at room temperature but tarnishes slowly in air. It reacts with oxygen, water vapor, and inorganic acids. Chemistry is dominated by the +5 state, with chemical similarity to tantalum and niobium.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Protactinium-231 (half-life 32,760 years) is the longest-lived and most common; it occurs in the uranium-235 decay chain. Protactinium-234 is a short-lived intermediate in the uranium-238 decay chain.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
